transcript: Hello, everyone. I'm Dr. David Perlmutter. We live in a world that really wants to convince us that we should basically live our lives come what may and that there will be wonderful ways to pull us out of our various types of problems that we get into. Well, as it relates to our brains, that just isn't the case. What makes a good brain go bad ultimately turns out to be a metabolic change, and we are in fact the architects of our brain's destiny in that we absolutely control our brain's metabolism by the lifestyle choices that we make each and every day. Let's explore this a bit further. I've decided to call this presentation Mind Over Metabolism because we really need to get our arms around the notion that what makes a good brain go bad, and even as it relates to Alzheimer's disease, are metabolic changes that occur within the brain. But how exciting it is that we've now seen headlines that there are breakthrough drugs, momentous breakthroughs in the treatment of Alzheimer's. We see this in November of 2022, that this new drug, Lekembe or Lecanumab, is hailed as a momentous breakthrough as per the BBC. Here in the Washington Post, the FDA giving full approval to the first drug that clearly but modestly slows Alzheimer's, even though when you read the small print, even it's administered every other week and it can cause brain bleeding and swelling. Well, that's not necessarily a good thing. So we're told this is the start of a new era, a wonderful new era in the treatment of Alzheimer's. Isn't that exciting? Let's look at the data and just see what all this excitement is about. From the New England Journal of Medicine, this is the drug Lekembe or Lecanumab, and this is their study published in January of 2023. Looking at 1,800 individuals, participants, half received the drug, half were placebo, an 18-month study. This is a drug designed to target beta amyloid, the so-called cause of Alzheimer's disease. This is a drug designed to target that drug because, as we all know, and I'm being facetious, that is the cause of Alzheimer's disease. When we look at the cognitive function during the eight-month period of time of the placebo group, the group that did not receive the drug, we see a pretty dramatic decline in 18 months. But in those receiving this Lekembe or Lecanumab drug, what did we see? Well, we actually saw a very similar decline in their cognitive function as well. When we look at the actual graph from the study, what we see is an interesting thing that, again, both the placebo group and the licanumab group do decline. And at the 15-month point, it's as if both groups basically fall off a cliff with a much more dramatic drop in cognitive function. Whether they're receiving this wonder drug or not, between 15 and 18 months, a dramatic loss of cognitive function. But be that as it may, the other part of the story that I just hinted to is that there are consequences of using this drug as an intervention. We call these hemorrhages in the brain and swelling the brain brain amyloid-related imaging abnormalities or ARIAS. And a significant increased risk of these changes in the brain, which can be devastating, in the group receiving the drug versus placebo. This is what these things look like. swelling in the brain, hemorrhaging into the brain, far more likely in the group treated with this amyloid-modifying type of drug, of which there are now several. The other thing that we weren't told with all this excitement and these breakthrough announcements is that there is brain shrinkage actually associated with the use of these drugs. Another research group looked at the MRI scans of the individuals in the studies and compared them and actually determined that there was a significant loss of brain volume caused by the anti-amyloid beta drugs. They actually went back and looked at the original researchers' data and images and found that, in fact, there was a dramatic loss of brain volume. Now, there are two classes of anti-amyloid drugs. There are the amyloid drugs that inhibit a certain enzyme that's involved in the production of beta amyloid. We call these secretase inhibitors. And there's another group of drugs targeting amyloid that target the actual amyloid that's existing in the brain by creating a group of what are called monoclonal antibodies that target that amyloid and help the brain get rid of it. Here are a couple of the names, Aduhelm and Lekembe. These are really in the spotlight these days for a number of reasons. We'll see why. And they looked at these patients who were on the drugs versus placebo, and they looked at the volumetric MRI data in various areas of the brain. And what they discovered was in those individuals using the secretase inhibitors there was significant loss of tissue in the brain's memory center called the hippocampus as well as in the entire brain in general. In those drugs that targeted the amyloid itself using antibody therapy there was a loss in brain mass such that the areas within the brain called the ventricles actually increased in size. And what they stated was that these mild cognitively impaired participants treated with these anti-amyloid drugs were projected to have a material regression towards brain volumes that were seen in Alzheimer's dementia eight months earlier than if they were untreated. So something we need to think about. This was the headline that appeared in MedPage today. The other thing that I think is really important as it relates to these drugs is, according to this new study appearing in the well-respected peer-reviewed journal Neurology, that when we look at who qualifies for the use of these drugs based upon, you know, making sure we don't have people with other medical problems, etc., that might be worsened by the use of these drugs, fewer than 10% of Alzheimer's patients in this real-world cohort would actually qualify for these drugs, costing $60,000 to $80,000 a year. But we do have the sudden news, a release of yet a third type of drug called Gatenumab in early Alzheimer's disease. This study just came out in the New England Journal of Medicine. Again, November of 2023, a very well-respected peer-reviewed journal. And how this drug works similarly is it's the creation of a monoclonal antibody, that's that three-winged purple image in the middle of the screen, that then targets, is directed against the existing amyloid in the brain with the notion that this is going to target that amyloid and rid the brain of amyloid because, again, that is thought to be the cause, if you will, of Alzheimer's disease again looked at about a thousand each of treated and untreated patients average age 70 years followed these people over 116 weeks and what did the study show first let's look at what happened in the brains as we did as we look at the PET scans of people that first the untreated group what happens to the amyloid levels in their brains, it goes up with time. What happens to the amyloid levels in the brains treated with the drug? A pretty robust decrease in amyloid in their brains. Well, if amyloid is causing this cognitive decline, we would expect to see a pretty significant difference then in the cognitive function comparing the placebo to the treatment group. What did these researchers find? As we look at the first trial, no difference in changes over time in their cognitive function, and the second trial, similarly, similarly no difference when we look at the composite chart graph comparing the intervention drug that get tenorimab through to placebo what do we see virtually no change in terms of their cognitive decline both groups decline quite dramatically despite the fact that one group had a significant lowering in their brains of beta amyloid. But here's what is also fundamentally important. When we look again at these amyloid-related imaging abnormalities or the brain hemorrhaging and swelling that occurs, a pretty dramatic difference. That's the placebo group and this is the treatment group. So I think when we have the notion of above all do no harm, primum non no ser, that this is something we should be thinking about, that we're not gaining any benefit here as you look at the risk-benefit ratio. There is no benefit, but look at what the risk may very well be. Dramatic increased risk in these brain hemorrhages and edema. These authors concluded that among persons with early Alzheimer's, the use of this new drug led to a lower amyloid plaque burden than placebo at 116 weeks, but was not associated with slower clinical decline as albert einstein has reminded us many times that the definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results well here's the latest news on one drug aduhelm that the company manufacturing it is going to stop its manufacturing and selling of this drug. And beyond that, quite interesting to note that the drug maker is going to stop developing and selling Aduhelm, an anti-amyloid monoclonal antibody that did receive FDA-accelerated approval in 2021, and they're also going to terminate the confirmatory trial, which was a requirement of the drug's accelerated approval. In other words, the drug got approved here in the United States by the FDA, although all of the studies confirming that it did anything had not been completed. That's kind of interesting. So where are we getting this messaging that we should be so dependent upon drugs? Well, let's see what the Alzheimer's Association is telling us, that there are 6.7 million Americans living with Alzheimer's dementia, that among people age 70, 61% of those with Alzheimer's are expected to die before the age of 80. So it's a life-threatening issue compared with only 30% of people without Alzheimer's dementia. And finally, and this is very important, remember this, that Alzheimer's begins 20 years or more before the memory loss and other symptoms then develop. And this is, taken from this organization. On the far left of your screen, a person experiences memory or thinking problems. On the far right of your screen, treatment is started as a person goes through the continuum. Let me just bring these together so it'll make it easier. The point I'm trying to make is that the cognitive concerns begin, and then ultimately at the end of the continuum you get treatment and on which is ongoing under the care of a specialist who is able to do these intravenous infusions of drugs that basically with all due respect do not work and are associated with significant morbidity and yes mortality so again this is the again, this is the continuum, and I'm going to challenge, I'm going to augment it now myself, and make room for what should be on the left side of your screen. I'm cognitively intact and plan to keep it that way. Thank you very much. Yes, the notion of prevention needs to be something we look at as it relates to Alzheimer's disease. Dr. Michael Merzenich, a good friend and who's been called the father of neuroplasticity, made us aware of really how ineffective these drugs are. Recently appearing a report and he commented saying that the trial of these drugs do not support the hypothesis that amyloid is the primary neuropathological agent underlying the progressive neurobehavioral decline in Alzheimer's disease. And furthermore, the more obvious path to winning the battle against this human scourge is prevention. This is so important because, as he made clear, 80 to 90% of prospective cases can be either delayed or prevented. 80 to 90% of a number that's 55 million on our planet today. So I want to take a step back then and look at what we understand is really going on in the brains of Alzheimer's patients, that it is a bioenergetic issue. It is a failure of the brain's ability to supply blood to itself and to use the fuel in that blood, which is glucose, to power its cell. It's failing as it relates to its bioenergetics. And this is not recent data. This dates back to October 2017, where they looked at the metabolism of the brain and indicated, getting back to an earlier slide, that this hypometabolism occurs decades before clinical manifestations of Alzheimer's and that individuals with insulin resistance or have difficulty using glucose or type 2 diabetes have an increased risk for developing Alzheimer's disease. Moreover, we may be able to characterize the brain metabolic fingerprints that may be used to offer patients and research participants more personalized therapeutic options. In other words, therapy that targets brain metabolism. Yes, in days gone by, we saw differences in the MRI scans comparing controls to Alzheimer's disease, but now using PET scan and even more recently MRI, we can see that the brains of Alzheimer's patients, even prior to the diagnosis of Alzheimer's, even when there's no clinical issue like memory failure, we can detect and predict who's going to have Alzheimer's based on the loss of energy utilization that is so obvious on these PET scans. When we look at even mild Alzheimer's, brain glucose uptake is significantly reduced and along the continuum of an older person versus mild cognitive impairment and full-blown Alzheimer's, a dramatic reduction in glucose uptake. So what we're seeing now is that journals like the Journal of the American Medical Association are letting us know that it's glucose and brain metabolism that is, as per this title, moving to center stage. As they indicate at the same time, researchers are exploring ways to slow or prevent dementia using drugs and, importantly for all of us, lifestyle modifications typically prescribed for metabolic disorders like diabetes or obesity. And these inquiries have new urgency as several anti-amyloid targeting therapy disease drugs have failed in clinical trials. So what are these lifestyle issues that they're talking about and where is the data that would indicate that there may be some relationship to memory decline for example? Well let's start looking at some of this research. Here's a study following individuals for 10 years looking at their lifestyle choices and noting that a healthy lifestyle was associated with a slower rate of memory decline in cognitively normal older adults, including people who are genetically susceptible to memory decline. A huge study of 30,000 individuals followed for 10 years, and yes, they even looked at whether or not they carried the so-called Alzheimer's gene or the APE4 allele. They did cognitive evaluations and assessed their activities over the course of this 10-year study, looking at things like what foods you eat how much exercise are you getting are you interacting with others are you interacting with things challenging your brain were you ever a smoker or did you quit and well how much alcohol you're consuming and what they found was really quite fascinating that the rate of cognitive decline memory function, specifically, over 10 years was dramatically increased on the right side of your screen in those who had unfavorable lifestyle choices. And these are people who do not carry the Alzheimer's gene. Those who did carry the Alzheimer's gene, look at that, same results, meaning that the gene really is less important than we had thought, that carrying the so-called APOE4 allele does not condemn an individual to having a cognitive decline, that lifestyle choices are so important. We don't have a lot of time to go through them all. I'm going to go through physical exercise. I think this is certainly something that's within everyone's grasp. And let's look at some of the data to help us understand what is the relationship between activating our muscles during exercise and brain in both health and disease and I think it's great to explore this because we know that exercise is good for us we know that exercise is good for the brain but how does it work what's going on so this is an interesting study report that makes it clear that our muscles are an endocrine organ. Think about that, that the muscles are an endocrine organ. They secrete chemicals that have activity throughout the body, much like other endocrine organs that you are familiar with, pituitary, the pineal, and thyroid, to name a few. When we recognize the muscles as an endocrine organ, we embrace the notion that we can activate that endocrine organ and have it do its job when we exercise. So when we exercise, we're activating muscles and muscles are now creating myokines. And what are myokines? These are chemicals or cytokines that are synthesized released by muscles during muscular contraction and they are the molecular mediators of the great things that happen to our bodies and our brains when we exercise and we now are embracing a notion of looking at all the things involved with these muscle chemicals and what they do as we talk about a new term called the myokinome. And the myokinome then looks at all of this activity of the various myokines like interleukin-6, which we used to characterize as being purely pro-inflammatory, ketone bodies, many of you have heard of being in a ketogenic diet, why that's good for the brainate irisin cathepsin b and of course brain derived neurotrophic factor being so good for the brain these are active these are active regulators of our focus which is the metabolism of the brain and importantly that these are increased in muscular activity even when there isn't inflammation. It increases the ability to use glucose or blood sugar, the breakdown of fat in muscle and the utilization of fatty acids in muscle, and importantly activates something called AMPK. This is a pathway that allows us to use fat as a fuel that allows us to grow new mitochondria or energy producers even in the brain so that helps our metabolism. So we're now looking at the notion of proper muscle to brain signaling as an important mechanism as it relates to brain health and that when we don't utilize this process, we may set ourselves up for brain disease. Exercise actually improves insulin sensitivity. And this was a study looking at sedentary or non-exercising adults who are overweight and obese, and it was a relatively small study, 14 women, 7 men, elevated body mass index, and they did a study of their brain utilization of glucose, brain metabolism, if you will, before and after giving them a blast of insulin in their nose and compared that to what happened when some of them did an exercise program. And let me just walk through again what the study involved. They each had a baseline functional MRI that looks at brain metabolism. Then they had a blast of insulin in their noses that does get into the brain. 30 minutes later, they underwent another functional MRI. So looking at the utilization or metabolism in a part of the brain called the putamen, a specific area that was studied here, shows that comparison group, this is the group not exercising, after the 30 minutes of receiving insulin, no real change in the activity in this particular part of the brain. But when we look at what happens in the group over the eight-week period, that exercise then got the insulin and then had a repeat functional MRI, a dramatic increase in how insulin works in the brain to increase metabolism. Another thing they looked at is this notion of functional connectivity, how one part of the brain relates to another part of the brain, the connectivity, the connections, pretty dramatic increased presence of functional connectivity in the group that exercised, and also doing a mental test called a trail making, the lower score, the better, the faster you're doing this study, this test that shows that their brains are working better, and even looking at metabolism, looking at how their mitochondria work, a dramatic increase in the exercisers in comparison to those who did not who still received the insulin but did not exercise from the Journal of the American Medical Association looking at 80,000 adults filed for seven years they put a wrist accelerometer on them and they wanted to determine in this group of 80,000 people who got dementia, what did they find? Dramatic association between risk of getting dementia and lower levels of exercise. But importantly, their quote, that there's no minimal threshold to the beneficial association of step counts with incident dementia, meaning any exercise is good, more is better. In this study, 9,800 steps per day was optimal for being in the lowest risk group for developing dementia. But again, any level of activity is going to benefit your brain. So we need to consider brain metabolism, which involves really two things. The utilization of sugar and, of course, its delivery in the first place, blood supply. Central to this is a chemical recently discovered in the brain and in the body. It's actually a gas called nitric oxide, abbreviated NO. And nitric oxide is actually a powerful mechanism whereby exercise does its beautiful stuff in the brain. That exercise increases our ability to use glucose and improves blood supply because it goes through this nitric oxide channel. Could nitric oxide be something that relates to Alzheimer's? Could we maybe just say no nitric oxide to Alzheimer's? In fact, we're now seeing studies that show a significant reduction in risk for developing Alzheimer's in drugs, in people who use drugs that increase blood supply by increasing nitric oxide. Viagra, a drug for erectile dysfunction, was designed to increase nitric oxide and therefore increase blood supply. It does so throughout the body, not just as it relates to erectile dysfunction. Nitric oxide, again, does two things. It improves glucose utilization by enhancing how insulin works, and it improves blood supply. Glucose utilization and vascular perfusion are the difference between a normal brain and the Alzheimer's brain, and nitric oxide is a central mechanism. Now, I'm certainly not advocating that we all begin taking this drug. We'll look at what we can do to improve nitric oxide in just a moment. This study looked at what nitric oxide bioavailability does on progressive changes in the body and in the brain with respect to Alzheimer's. Looking at cerebral blood flow, young on the left and end-stage Alzheimer's on the right, dramatic reduction in blood flow to the brain. One might assume that perhaps they don't have as much nitric oxide. We'll get there in a moment. When we look at blood flow in the internal carotid artery that brings blood into the brain, dramatic reduction in going from young to old and to end stage on the far right of your screen, Alzheimer's. Looking at blood supply to the cortex, the top of the brain, which we can measure now. Again, the same decline. And we look at nitric oxide availability a very very interesting similar curve that we see this strong correlation of reduction of blood flow with reduction in nitric oxide bioavailability in the brain as we age but certainly worse as it relates to developing the development rather of end-stage Alzheimer's. So these authors demonstrated that in patients with Alzheimer's, that these changes are directly associated with Alzheimer's and not to aging, that nitric oxide bioavailability is the link between cardiovascular and central nervous systems, degenerative processes in patients at different levels of severity of Alzheimer's, again, related to the depletion of nitric oxide. Nitric oxide plays a central role in brain metabolism. Where does it come from? A lot of it is from recycling nitric oxide and those chemicals that make it, but a lot of it, actually 50%, comes from foods that are rich in something called nitrate. Nitrate-rich foods are converted by bacteria in the mouth to nitrite, and that becomes nitric oxide. I recently did an interview with a Dr. Mark Berhenne that's available on YouTube that covers this. That interview went viral because I think the way he explained this relationship and the important role of our mouth bacteria was really very stunning. So again, he is calling attention, as many have, to the role of the bacteria in the mouth. Well, we're told that we want to do everything we can to kill the bacteria in our mouth, right? So we don't have problems like, who knows what, bad breath and tooth decay. But is there a relationship, if we kill these bacteria, between glucose utilization and vascular perfusion? In other other words could killing off these bacteria have systemic issues could using mouthwash for example be related to risk for diabetes and in this really fascinating study from 2017 these researchers looked at about a thousand individuals overweight obese adults non. How often do you use mouthwash? They followed for three years. What did they find? Using mouthwash two or more times a day associated with a 49% increased risk of prediabetes or diabetes. What about high blood pressure? If nitric oxide is involved in blood pressure regulation, a very similar study, 540 individuals, again, three years, risk of developing high blood pressure, a powerful risk factor for Alzheimer's, risk of high blood pressure, 117% increase. And that's just from two times daily. So what else should we consider as we think about what's going on? How do we increase nitrate and then nitrite and nitric oxide by eating nitrate-rich foods, colorful vegetables like beets? We know that we can actually change the bacteria in the mouth. They respond with higher levels of nitrate. Again, the notion is if we eat a nitrate rich foods in this study a dramatic reduction in blood pressure by using a specific supplement that reduced rather that improved nitric oxide by giving nitrate bacteria converted to nitrite your body turns it into nitric oxide that lowers blood pressure. One of the pioneers is Dr. Nathan Bryan who has done a variety of studies demonstrating dramatic changes in patients with cardiovascular disease in individuals who received a pharmaceutical way orally of increasing their nitrate and therefore nitric oxide production. I had the opportunity to meet with him and I will be with him actually beginning a study of increasing nitric oxide as a way of treating individuals with existing Alzheimer's disease by, for the very first time, targeting their metabolism. Well, I hope you enjoyed that presentation. Again, metabolism is central to brain health and resistance to things like Alzheimer's disease. Metabolism is highly influenced by the various lifestyle choices we make each and every day. Thank you. Bye-bye.
